EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough that dug across the interior West Saturday and Sunday, |aligned with the left exit region of a meridional jet favoring large scale ascent across the Sierra and western Nevada. This setup for strong winds and widespread precipitation across the region. This included the potential for heavy Sierra snowfall with light snow accumulation on valley floors. As the first snow arrived Saturday AM wind observations over Sierra ridges showed gusts ranging 70-80 mph. This system was considered the coldest and most impactful storm up to this point this season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (WLKNV-NV DOT) located 6.1 mi NW of Hawthorne, NV, and mesonet station( EW0588 Yerington) located 5.0 ENE of Yerington, NV, reported a peak wind gust of 77 mph and 64 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was allowed to tap into Pacific moisture for a longer period of time that nudged it just enough north to make a more direct landfall into the central OR coast.  This next storm Friday evening into Saturday brought more gusty winds, Sierra snow, and valley rain/snow showers. This storm���s warmer nature raised snow levels a bit higher(5500-6500 feet) with the potential for mixed precipitation in the valley areas and foothills. The lower-end ensemble snow scenarios showed a bit more confident projection for at least a foot of snow in the Sierra with potential for 18-24 inches along the Sierra crest. Regardless, this contributed to another bout of travel disruptions, especially for ski commutes during the upcoming holiday weekend. Wind speeds have trended up a bit with this storm as well with the potential for wind gusts 40 to 50 mph for the valley areas. Sierra ridge wind gusts got a boost with potential for gusts 90+ mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter(WA-12) located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village, and the public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported 24 hour storm snowfall totals of 4.5 inches and 4.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger and colder more classic winter storm followed Wednesday through|early Thursday, bringing additional wind and snow impacts to the Sierra and western NV. This storm system���s trajectory allowed it to drop southward into eastern California and western Nevada Wednesday-Wednesday night. This storm started with a short round of strong winds followed by bursts of heavier snow. Although this storm appeared to have a more overland trajectory with liquid precipitation totals a little lower, the forcing from this low and attendant cold front remained sufficiently intact to produce additional snow with at least an even chance of 6 or more inches in the Tahoe basin southward to western Mono County, and an additional foot or more along the Sierra crest. Plenty of winter travel hazards at all elevations continued into the Thursday AM commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit  reported a peak wind gust of 98 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slider-type shortwave and attendant moisture were forecast to bring a modest |with Sierra snowfall staying on the lighter side compared to previous February winter storms. Snowfall totals along the Sierra crest were expected to vary from 3-6 inches with lower elevation Sierra communities (Truckee, South Lake Tahoe, Bridgeport, Mammoth Lakes) seeing only 1-3 inches through Thursday morning. There was a 10% chance for these locales seeing up to 6 inches of snow in a boom scenario. Bands of moderate snowfall with this winter system in fact did bring a modest ���Boom��� storm total snowfall to some areas south of US-50 to include the Eastern Sierra, Mono County, southern Lyon County, and Mineral County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit  reported a peak wind gust of 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warmer storm brought more rain, high elevation snow, and gusty southwesterly winds late Tuesday into Wednesday. An upper-level low moving into the Pacific Northwest flattened a persistent ridge on Tuesday afternoon, and allowed warmer subtropical moisture to be transported into our region with the increased more zonally directed westerly flow aloft. Higher ridge level winds increased late Tuesday morning as an upper jet oriented over the region bringing peak overnight wind gusts to around 80-100 mph. Ridge winds remained elevated through Wednesday, with breezy SW winds mixing down to valleys by Wednesday afternoon. The main precipitation band moved into the region by Tuesday PM wrapping up by late Wednesday AM. Snow levels remained a bit higher than previous storms due to the subtropical tap. Snow levels will hovered between 8000-8500 feet during the main precipitation event, briefly topping upwards to 9000 feet at times. Snow accumulations were confined to the high Sierra, with predicted storm totals ranging 2-6 inches above 8000 feet. In terms of liquid totals, the highest values expected stayed along the Sierra crest, generally between 0.75-1 inches. A sharp gradient in liquid precipitation persisted east of the crest due to stronger rain shadowing with 0.25-0.5 inches generally west of US-395 and trace-0.1 inch east of Highway 395.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (WASNV-NV DOT)) located 3.2 mi S  of Washoe City, NV, and mesonet station (SRNNV-Lakeview ) located 3.7 mi NW of Lakeview, NV, both reported peak wind gusts of 69 mph.

This latest storm system brought  impacts to travel over Sierra passes to include mountain communities Wednesday night through Thursday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit  reported a peak wind gust of 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather returns Wednesday afternoon to close out the month of January. Therefore, strong and gusty winds especially over higher mountain elevations was followed with mountain snow and valley rain showers. Wind was the first hazard expected starting over the Sierra and NE CA, then spreading into western Nevada with south winds between 30-50 mph, and gusts over 60-80 mph across higher mountain ridges and passes. Valley winds were projected to be 15-35 mph with gusts up to 55 mph. The probability of 60 mph winds across mountain areas in CA was 60% or greater and 10-20% over W NV. Lake winds were around 15-25 mph with gusts to 35-45 mph.||Valley rain and mountain snow showers were projected to move into the region   Wednesday PM. Global models and their respective ensemble members initially dug the trough and a deeper layer of sub-tropical IVT moisture will quickly sweep east and south across portions of northeast California, before descending into the Sierra, western Nevada, and Mono Co. Snow levels started near 7000 feet on Wednesday, then dropped to 6000 feet by Thursday. Most higher elevation areas received mostly rain initially before transitioning to mostly snow Thursday evening. Snow levels dropped to 5500 ft on Thursday afternoon, and dropped further to below 4000 feet by Friday morning. The Sierra above 7000 and Mono Co were given even chances for about 1-2 feet of new snow with 6-12 inches below 7000 feet. For northeast California 4-10 inches of accumulated snow, and up to 20 inches in the higher mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit  reported a peak wind gust of 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Attention then turned to a significant winter storm that brought widespread snow impacts across the Sierra and western Nevada Sunday into early next week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ongoing winter system continued to impact the Sierra with travel impacts across Sierra passes with areas of intermittent snow showers to continue  through Saturday across the Sierra.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported a 24 hour storm total snowfall of 6.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds and periods of rain and mountain snow showers arrived on the 22nd and lingered through the 24th. IDSS focused on Sierra travel impacts through the  weekend. Unsettled weather was favored to continue through the following week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CO-OP observer located at Incline Village, and the public at the Mount Rose Ski Base, NV, reported 24 hour storm snowfall totals of 11.5 inches, and 10 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system provided periods of gusty winds, rain and mountain snow, and cooler temperatures on the 12th that continued through the weekend. Ensemble guidance projected a deep cutoff low pressure dropping south along the west coast through the 13th. This low then moved into central CA by the 14th, and into the central Great Basin by 15th and brought several days of cooler temperatures, gusty winds, and precipitation throughout the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS(KLOL) located 9.0 mi SSW of Lovelock, NV, at Derby Field, NV, reported a thunderstorm peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather returned on the 4th with a storm system that brought gusty winds along with valley rain and mountain snow. The storm trajectory allowed a quick moving cold front to sweep across the region early morning on the 4th. This front set the region up to see two impactful weather concerns: gusty to strong southwesterly winds along with winter-like precipitation that included accumulated mountain snow and valley rain. Conditions slowly improved on  the 5th with a slow warming trend and low precipitation chances following early the next week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter (WA-12) located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village reported 24 hour snowfall totaling 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional snow chances continued mid-week, mostly for the northern Sierra. Conditions continued to be treacherous over the Sierra overnight on the 4th with breezy winds, snow, and blowing snow. Conditions briefly improved over the Sierra the morning through the afternoon of the 5th with additional snow shower chances continuing thereafter, especially for the Sierra north of US-50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located the Mount Rose Ski Base, a trained spotter (WA-12) located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village, and the public at the Diamond Peak Ski Base, NV, reported 24 hour storm snowfall totals of 12.0 inches, 9.0 inches, and 7 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By mid week, after a rather tranquil start to the New Year, a weak occluded low split from the broader trough then elongated as it drove south west of the Sierra crest limiting QPF totals over our region. Guidance showed that frontogenetic forcing, however, would amplify in central Nevada, and stretch west into the eastern Sierra and Mono County. The enhanced lift, coupled with precipitation banding, as projected by EC EFI/SOT and blended guidance showed a snowfall ���Boom��� potential  for at least moderate snow south of US-50 in western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported a 24 hour storm total snowfall of 5.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low moved into the pacific northwest leading to gusty winds, lowering snow levels, and areas of heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches fell at site (BKLN2) Buckskin Lower Snotel, Elevation 6929 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of winter storms that was directed a bit more south of Lake Tahoe along the eastern Sierra into Mono county. This system brought additional snow amounts to much of the Sierra late Sunday into Monday with periods of lower elevation/valley rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds. The main area of focus shifted from the Tahoe basin to Mono county where the White Mountains looked to get 2-3 inches of new snow while Mammoth Mountain was projected to receive another 5-10 inches of fresh snow. Snow levels hovered around 7000 feet for much of this second winter storm, then lowered to near 6000 feet towards the tail end of the storm late Monday. ECMWF EFI signals for CAPE across western Nevada showed higher instability with this second storm that indicated a better spillover potential and for showers to  persist well into Monday afternoon and evening. Storm liquid precipitation totals with this storm was projected to be about 1.50-2.00 inches along the span of the Sierra crest that translated to another 1-2 plus feet of snowfall for elevations above 8000 feet with this second storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Summit, and a trained spotter located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village reported 48 hour storm total snowfall of 10.00 inches and 3.5 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of storms pushed into the Sierra on Friday night, with periods of rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds persisting into early the following week. High confidence followed for additional snow accumulations above 6500 feet, that included impacts to the Sierra passes overnight Friday into Saturday. Liquid water totals Friday PM through Sunday AM ranged from 0.20 to 0.75 inches along the eastern Sierra crest of Mono County to 0.60 to 2.00 inches farther north. Spillover precipitation east of the crest was limited with this storm, with 0.50 to 0.75 inches in the Tahoe Basin and a few hundredths to 0.50 inches along Highway 395. This equated to 8-16 inches of snowfall along the Sierra crest of Mono County to 8-18 inches along the crest farther north towards the Lake Tahoe basin. Although there was a lack of a significant wind signal during the period of both storms, ridge winds around the area still managed to approach 80+ mph. Otherwise, locally breezy conditions prevailed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 3.0 mi WSW of the Mount Rose Summit reported 24 hour storm total snowfall of 6.0 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicular accident caused 1 death, as road conditions appear icy after Winter Storm. Sky conditions appear clear at the time of accident with air temperatures near 0F (zero Fahrenheit) at closest observation site. The photo in the article shows the road looks like the surface is compromised with snow or slush. This likely lead to the driver to lose control of the vehicle and to travel across the northbound travel lane onto the northbound shoulder and overturn on an embankment. This occurred on US-95 near MM 5 in Humboldt County about 5 miles north of Winnemucca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excess monsoonal moisture from the late July four corners high pressure remained over Nevada in early August due to the high pressure system destabilizing, causing weak upper flow. Hot temperatures, increase in lift and instability with the excess moisture helped formed thunderstorms in the afternoons.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Nevada BLM Officials surveyed flood damage caused by a slow moving thunderstorm at the Dolly Varden Mountain Range. The survey provided evidence of flooding from water runoffs onto a service road caused by a thunderstorm over the area that lasted 2.5 hours that created a deep trench and water overflow over the service road at the range. Estimated radar precipitation between 1.5 to 2 inches of rain. Pictures of the survey and coordinates are attached.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate stream north of mid-level monsoonal moisture coupled with instability and daytime heating enabled a 40-60% chance for thunderstorms during this brief period. Storms initiated over Mono County afternoon of the 2nd and worked their way north into northeast CA into the evening. There was a 40% chance of nocturnal thunderstorms across far western NV and northeast CA. Storms were projected to have the potential to produce localized flash flooding under stronger, training storm cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (FOXN2-Fox Mountain) located 27.0 mi NNW of Gerlach, NV, and a mesonet station (JSPN2-Juniper Springs) located 36.0 mi S of Vya, NV, reported total rainfall amounts of 0.58 and 0.84 inches respectively.

Guidance showed that frontogenetic forcing, however, would amplify in central Nevada, and stretch west into the eastern Sierra and Mono County. The enhanced lift, coupled with precipitation banding, as projected by EC EFI/SOT and blended guidance showed a snowfall ���Boom��� potential  for at least moderate snow south of US-50 in western NV.

Another Samaritan reported that they recorded a 64 mph gust, their neighbors tree blew down, and that there was damage to a barn.   Quima Peak Raws site, Elevation 7984 feet, located within 25 miles of Carvers, NV reported wind gusts of 50-57 mph between 2 and 3 PM. The winds would however get stronger later on, where the same site measured a 64 mph wind gust at 7:11 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of winter storms that was directed a bit more south of Lake Tahoe along the eastern Sierra into Mono county. This system brought additional snow amounts to much of the Sierra late Sunday into Monday with periods of lower elevation/valley rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds. The main area of focus shifted from the Tahoe basin to Mono county where the White Mountains looked to get 2-3 inches of new snow while Mammoth Mountain was projected to receive another 5-10 inches of fresh snow. Snow levels hovered around 7000 feet for much of this second winter storm, then lowered to near 6000 feet towards the tail end of the storm late Monday. ECMWF EFI signals for CAPE across western Nevada showed higher instability with this second storm that indicated a better spillover potential and for showers to  persist well into Monday afternoon and evening. Storm liquid precipitation totals with this storm was projected to be about 1.50-2.00 inches along the span of the Sierra crest that translated to another 1-2 plus feet of snowfall for elevations above 8000 feet with this second storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Summit, and a trained spotter located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village reported 48 hour storm total snowfall of 10.00 inches and 3.5 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level instability lead to cu-buildups and some more organized thunderstorms across Humboldt and Lander. These storms produce gusty outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (UP972) Ebeow1 UPR, Elevation 4735 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This comparatively weaker winter storm opened up another active weather pattern that tracked through the Sierra and western Nevada late in the evening February 17-18. This storm brought higher elevation snowfall that was heavy at times above 7000 feet. This storm also included the possibility of an additional several feet of snowfall.  Mainly spillover rainfall fell over lower western Nevada valleys that included periods of gusty winds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE-DRI) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit  reported a peak wind gust of 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger and colder more classic winter storm followed Wednesday through|early Thursday, bringing additional wind and snow impacts to the Sierra and western NV. This storm system���s trajectory allowed it to drop southward into eastern California and western Nevada Wednesday-Wednesday night. This storm started with a short round of strong winds followed by bursts of heavier snow. Although this storm appeared to have a more overland trajectory with liquid precipitation totals a little lower, the forcing from this low and attendant cold front remained sufficiently intact to produce additional snow with at least an even chance of 6 or more inches in the Tahoe basin southward to western Mono County, and an additional foot or more along the Sierra crest. Plenty of winter travel hazards at all elevations continued into the Thursday AM commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 1.0 mi SE of Silver Springs, NV, 5.0 mi ESE of Fernley, NV, and in Stagecoach, NV, reported 24 hour storm snowfall totals of 18.0  inches, 17.5 inches,  and 9.0 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning showers and thunderstorms produced a funnel cloud that was observed looking NE from Spring Creek, NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported photos on social media of a funnel cloud looking NE from Spring Creek during morning showers and thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of storms pushed into the Sierra on Friday night, with periods of rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds persisting into early the following week. High confidence followed for additional snow accumulations above 6500 feet, that included impacts to the Sierra passes overnight Friday into Saturday. Liquid water totals Friday PM through Sunday AM ranged from 0.20 to 0.75 inches along the eastern Sierra crest of Mono County to 0.60 to 2.00 inches farther north. Spillover precipitation east of the crest was limited with this storm, with 0.50 to 0.75 inches in the Tahoe Basin and a few hundredths to 0.50 inches along Highway 395. This equated to 8-16 inches of snowfall along the Sierra crest of Mono County to 8-18 inches along the crest farther north towards the Lake Tahoe basin. Although there was a lack of a significant wind signal during the period of both storms, ridge winds around the area still managed to approach 80+ mph. Otherwise, locally breezy conditions prevailed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located 3.0 mi WSW of the Mount Rose Summit reported 24 hour storm total snowfall of 6.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather returned on the 4th with a storm system that brought gusty winds along with valley rain and mountain snow. The storm trajectory allowed a quick moving cold front to sweep across the region early morning on the 4th. This front set the region up to see two impactful weather concerns: gusty to strong southwesterly winds along with winter-like precipitation that included accumulated mountain snow and valley rain. Conditions slowly improved on  the 5th with a slow warming trend and low precipitation chances following early the next week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter (WA-12) located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village reported 24 hour snowfall totaling 9.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system provided periods of gusty winds, rain and mountain snow, and cooler temperatures on the 12th that continued through the weekend. Ensemble guidance projected a deep cutoff low pressure dropping south along the west coast through the 13th. This low then moved into central CA by the 14th, and into the central Great Basin by 15th and brought several days of cooler temperatures, gusty winds, and precipitation throughout the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit, reported a peak wind gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This first of two impactful weather systems arrived in the area during afternoon on the 27th with the passage of a cold front. Gusty to strong winds across the region as well as valley rain and mountain snow accompanied this initial storm system through the morning of the 28th. Following a brief, a second impactful system brought additional precipitation chances for mountain snow and valley rain that started overnight on the 29th that continued through the Easter weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE) located 0.9 mi E of Mount Rose Summit reported a peak wind gust of 91 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of mountain snow with a valley rain and snow mix will continued through the holiday weekend with this second of two impactful late season storms. This system brought travel regional impacts especially over Sierra mountain passes. The low pressure system that rotated cyclonically just off-shore the central CA coast enabled a south-southwest upper flow to tap into a thick layer of Pacific moisture that spread north and northeast into the Sierra and western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base, the public located at the  Diamond Peak Ski Base, a trained spotter (WA-12) located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village, and a CoCoRAHS station (NV-CC-14) located 4.4 mi N of Carson City, NV) reported storm total snowfall totals of 25.0 inches, 23 inches and 6.2 inches respectively.

Expected impacts included slick roads, especially during the nighttime/early morning commute hours in the Sierra and surrounding foothill areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An exceptionally prolonged and intense heat wave broke many daily and even all-time temperature records. Forty-two people died in Las Vegas of heat-related causes during and immediately after the heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought locally heavy rain and a flash flood potential along with strong outflow gusts through Wednesday. Abundant moisture and instability were present as noted by the 0.81 PWAT reading from the 23 July 1200Z KREV sounding this morning. Sufficient clearing also helped temperatures to climb well above average for late July. Short term high-resolution HREF guidance showed an uptick in PWATs Wednesday as deeper moisture expanded northward across western NV and far northeast CA |as a ridge axis slid east with upper level southerly flow increasing ahead of a trough advancing into the Pacific Northwest. A few severe storms with gusts to 60 mph and nickel sized hail were forecasted on 23 July. But flash flooding would be the bigger concern as storms moved either slower or anchored near steep terrain and burn scars. For 24 July, mid level S/SW winds increased which increased both storm motions and shear for storm organization. SPC introduced a marginal risk for severe wind gusts near the Oregon border with similar severe weather threats for stronger outflows and blowing dust reaching the Basin and Range. While storms were expected to move faster, training storm  cells would continue to bring a heightened threat for flash flooding for areas south of I-80 with focus on the eastern Sierra into southern Lyon and Mineral counties. For 25 July, drier and more stable air confined storms to the Basin and Range where activity was forecast to be isolated but still able to produce gusty outflows and areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 0.25 mi SW of Sparks, NV, of ponding water over the roadway causing a portion of Rock Blvd to be temporarily closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure system to the south of Nevada created a weak flow pattern over the area that still had excess moisture from monsoonal storm pattern earlier in the week which elevated conditions to produce thunderstorms, some reaching severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (COIN2) Coils Creek, Elevation 6745 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 13 July, forcing provided by an upper low off the central CA coast, and a southerly flow aloft riding around the outer western periphery of an upper ridge centered over Four-Corners region pulled deep monsoonal moisture northward into the Sierra and western Nevada. These conditions initiated scattered showers and thunderstorms across much of the region. As these weather features showed little movement, a near repeat scenario occurred for 14 July. Showers with embedded storms and lightning lingered through the night into the morning hours. The return of a southwest flow that evening across the Sierra kicked the storms into west central NV with the activity winding down later that night. Residual moisture was slower to scour out the afternoon of 15 July south of US-50 that resulted in a few short lived showers and initiation of pulse-type thunderstorms. The typical array of storm effects included locally heavy rainfall, lightning, outflow gusts of 35+ knots, small hail, and lower CIGS/VIS with mountain obscurations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (Luning-NV DOT) located 10.0 mi WNW of Luning, NV, reported  a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated shower and thunderstorm chances returned 20 July, and remained mostly south of Highway 50 with Mono-Mineral Counties being the focal points for activity. High-resolution ensemble guidance also showed the best chances for showers and thunderstorms mostly south of highway 50. Simulations projected a 30-40% chance for isolated showers and a few storms that would initiate mostly across Mono, southern Lyon, and Mineral counties around 1-2PM. There was also a chance (10-20%) that one or two storms would push northeast into eastern Churchill County as well. Storms quickly tapered off in the evening after sundown. Afternoon west-southwest breezes of 20-30 mph were expected to be slightly enhanced  in the vicinity of developing storms. The usual storm related culprits of copious lightning, as well as gusty and erratic outflows accompanied storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station SRZNV (NV-DOT) located 4.6 mi NE of Schurz, NV, reported  a peak wind gust of 75 mph.

For 25 July, drier and more stable air confined storms to the Basin and Range where activity was forecast to be isolated but still able to produce gusty outflows and areas of blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northern transport of moisture around the periphery of the ridge building over the Great Basin region, along with increased low level convergence ahead of an incoming upper trough, increased the showers and Thunderstorm chances across the Sierra from Mono County to the Lake Tahoe basin as well as east and north across western Nevada. The surge of monsoonal moisture pushed PWAT values upwards of 0.7-1.0 that were close to the 95th percentile for this time of the season. Storms were projected to have the potential for localized heavy rainfall in addition to gusty and erratic outflow winds, and lightning that raised new fire start concerns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated shower and thunderstorm chances returned 20 July, and remained mostly south of Highway 50 with Mono-Mineral Counties being the focal points for activity. High-resolution ensemble guidance also showed the best chances for showers and thunderstorms mostly south of highway 50. Simulations projected a 30-40% chance for isolated showers and a few storms that would initiate mostly across Mono, southern Lyon, and Mineral counties around 1-2PM. There was also a chance (10-20%) that one or two storms would push northeast into eastern Churchill County as well. Storms quickly tapered off in the evening after sundown. Afternoon west-southwest breezes of 20-30 mph were expected to be slightly enhanced  in the vicinity of developing storms. The usual storm related culprits of copious lightning, as well as gusty and erratic outflows accompanied storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County official reported 4.0 mi WSW of Lockwood, NV, road closures between Hidden Highlands Drive and Sleepy Hollow Drive due to flash flooding in the area of Hidden Valley. About 100 homes were affected by the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low that moved inland the afternoon and evening of the 18th brought increased rain, snow above 8500 feet, and thunder chances to the region on the 19th. Higher resolution ensemble simulations also continued to project chances for showers and isolated thunderstorms across the Sierra, the Sierra Front and vicinity as the upper low swung southeast towards southern Nevada. The best dynamics with this system remained mostly northward of the low pressure center with a jet streak located in the vicinity of the Eastern Sierra. High-Resolution Rapid Response guidance consistently showed concurrence with this scenario. Differences noted in ensemble simulations, however, lead to some uncertainty with the intensity of shower activity. Therefore, the expected intensities would range between light to moderate rainfall with isolated areas of heavy rainfall. Simulations showed that Mono County and the Eastern Sierra would have the best chances for higher elevation snowfall above 8500 with blended guidance showing a 20-50% chance for storm total snow greater than 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations (2306D) located at Incline Village, NV,  a Trained Spotter located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village, NV, and a mesonet station (SW0254) located 1.0 mi NE of Crystal Bay, NV, reported 24hr rainfall storm totals of 0.78 inches, 0.62 inches, and 0.56 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Sept. 7, 2024, the Davis Fire broke out in the afternoon in the area of Davis Creek Regional Park located 20 miles south of Reno, Nevada. This wind-driven fire rapidly burned a total of 5,824 acres of private, state, and federal lands, destroying two commercial buildings, 14 residences, and 22 outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Sept. 7, 2024, the Davis Fire broke out around 2:30 p.m. in the area of Davis Creek Regional Park located 20 miles south of Reno, Nevada. This wind-driven fire rapidly burned a total of 5,824 acres of private, state, and federal lands, destroying two commercial buildings, 14 residences, and 22 outbuildings. Truckee Meadows Fire Protection District, Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest, and Bureau of Land Management managed the fire under a unified command. Due to the fire���s complexity, the Southwest Area Incident Management Team 1 was order and took over the management of the fire the evening of Monday, Sept. 9, and transferred it back to a local Type 4 organization on Thursday, Sept. 18. The fire was officially called 100 percent contained at 6 p.m. on Wednesday, Sept. 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Guidance showed an upper level low opening into a trough and passing over northern California into western Nevada during the evening hours the 2nd. The attendant cold front produced gusty southwest winds throughout the day into the evening. Ridgetop wind gusts were projected to increased to around 50-70 mph by the morning of the 2nd, and last through the day and overnight. These winds allowed for critical fire weather conditions, choppy waters on area lakes, aviation impacts, and blowing dust near desert areas. Higher resolution model guidance also suggested the potential for showers and thunderstorms along and east of US-95 during the afternoon hours of the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passing to the north of the region, tightened pressure and thermal gradient across the region that resulted in wind gusts up to 45 mph with higher gusts across higher terrain, especially up along the Oregon border. This created impacts to recreation, fire weather, and aviation. These conditions required issuance of a Red Flag Warning for NE-CA and NW-NV Friday, as well as issuance of Lake Wind Advisories for Lake Tahoe and Pyramid Lake late morning through early evening Friday. Increased turbulence early Friday morning resulted in bumpy commercial flights locally to include potential for areas of blowing dust, and low visibilities, along Hwy 50, Hwy 95, and I-80 out east in the Basin and Range. This included a slight (10-15%) chance of rain showers Friday in Mono and Mineral counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (RGX) located on Virginia Peak, NV, mesonet station (WSVNV-NV NDOT) located 1.3 mi SW of Verdi, NV, and mesonet station (Slide) located 0.9 E of the Mount Rose Summit, NV, reported  peak wind gusts of 74 mph, 71 mph, and 67 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm ushered in cooler temperatures, as well as our initial round of showers, including snow showers across the highest terrain. A cold front that pushed across northern CA, and southern OR, early morning on the 15th spread the shower potential southward along the front with precipitation starting in western Nevada by mid-morning, and the eastern Sierra by the early afternoon. Showers persisted into the rest of central/eastern Nevada through the early evening. Blended guidance shows fairly high thunderstorm probabilities with 15-20% across the Basin and Range into western Nevada, and the highest chances (20-30%) across the Tahoe Basin and northeastern CA. Storm totals remained around 0.05-0.25 for much of the area, especially for northern Washoe and Lassen counties. Lower precipitation amounts (around 0.10 or less) were forecasted for areas south of I-80 in Mono and Mineral counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (MJBN2-Majuba) located 21.0 mi WNW of Imlay, NV, reported 24 hour rainfall  totaling 0.55 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought more impactful weather to the region starting on Sunday, the 27th, that continued into the beginning of the work week. Forecast guidance showed a Pacific Northwest trough making its way across western CONUS with the trough axis passing over the CWA on late Monday afternoon. This pattern aloft allowed a quick moving cold front to bring gusty southwest winds during the day on Sunday, 26th October, and wintry precipitation chances on that continued into through the early part of the week. The latest NBM probabilities for wind gusts of 40 mph or greater on the 26th showed the Lake Tahoe Basin having around a 40-60% chance of occurrence with the Reno area seeing around a 30-40% chance. A Lake Wind Advisory was therefore issued for Lake Tahoe and Pyramid Lake for Sunday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (Slide) located 0.9 E of the Mount Rose Summit, NV, a mesonet station (RGX-Virginia Peak) located 8.0 mi SW of Nixon, NV, and mesonet station (WASNV-Washoe City) located 3.0 mi SW of New Washoe City, NV, peak wind gusts of 72 mph, 65 mph, and 63 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This stronger of a one-two shot of storm systems advanced an east and south moving cold front inside slider-like mainly near and north of US-50. The bands of showers, and embedded thunder, also included very small hail as well. This front brought gusty northerly winds (30-40+ mph), choppy conditions especially for Pyramid Lake, and sharply colder temperatures. Shower and storm activity peaked during the mid-late afternoon, and then winded down as it moved south|of US-50 by early this evening. Snow levels also dropped quickly to near lake level around the Lake Tahoe Basin early evening with a few snow showers remaining in the mix as well. Farther south into Mono County, a short period of upslope flow behind the front lead to minor snow accumulations near and|west of US-395. The combination of the amplified northerly flow aloft, and persistent cold air advection allowed a few sites to receive up to 3 of additional lake-effect snowfall enhancement on larger lakes like Mono Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (LKSN2-Stateline),  located 1.0 mi NNW of Stateline, NV, reported a peak wind gust of 103 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season cold front from a low pressure system moving across Nevada increased the chance for scattered showers that initiated during the early morning hours of August 25th. The heaviest showers were projected to stay mostly north of I-80 with a 50-60% chance of showers along the Sierra crest that extended south to Mammoth Lakes. There was also a 20-30% chance of thunderstorms as well. Some showers along the Sierra above 9000' had the potential to bring a dusting of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MRSN2) located at the Mount Rose Summit (SNOTEL) reported 24hr rainfall storm totals of 0.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief lull on the 15th, this third in the ongoing trio of storm systems pushed quickly through the region on the 16th and early on the 17th. This storm system reached northeast California early morning on the 16th, impacted the Tahoe Basin by mid-late morning through the afternoon of that same date, and diminished as it tracked south into Mono County that evening. The primary impact with this storm were the gusty winds that increased to 80 mph over Sierra with projected gusts of 30-40 mph in the lower elevation Sierra valleys that mixed down into western Nevada. Snowfall was limited, however, due to its speed and timing during mostly daylight hours. But bursts of moderate to heavy snowfall with rates ranging 0.5-1.0 inch per hour between Lassen County south into the Tahoe Basin. Most snow impacts were concentrated over mountain passes as snow levels started at around 5000 to 6000 feet, then increased to 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 4.0 mi W of Gardnerville Ranch, NV, reported 24 hour storm total rainfall of 0.54 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm arrived on the 21st, and provided light rain, high elevation snow, and strong gusty winds. This later system appeared to be the most impactful as southwest to west winds approached 35-45 mph across lower elevation Sierra communities and the Sierra Front morning through the afternoon time period on the 21st with wind gusts exceeding 90 mph along exposed Sierra ridgetops. This storm system brought additional impacts to recreation, and brought bumpy flights into and out of regional airports. Rain and snow totals were relatively meager with this storm with accumulated snowfall of only 1-4 inches confined to elevations above 7000-7500 feet on the night of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (LVYN2-Little Valley) located 2.0 miles ESE of Washoe, NV,  a mesonet station (SVPN2-Carson City) located 4.0 miles NNE of Carson City, NV, and a mesonet station (NV016-Mount Rose Highway) located 1.0 mile ESE of Mount Rose Summit, reported peak wind gusts of 73 mph, 91 mph, and 101 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger storm arrived Sierra and western Nevada during the night of 23rd that brought gusty winds, valley rain, and heavier mountain snowfall through Christmas Eve. Snowfall totals trended upwards, but chances of 6+ inches and 12+inches of snow along the Sierra crest by Christmas morning remained in excess of 70% and 40%, respectively. Meanwhile, there was a 20-30% chance that local foothills to include Virginia City would see at least a dusting of snow. Gusty southwest to west winds returned late on the 23rd into Christmas Eve with most locales having a 40% or greater chance for gusts reaching 30+ mph. As such, there remained high confidence that impacts to recreation and travel will unfold on Christmas Eve.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (NV021-SW Gardnerville), located 2.0 miles S of Minden, NV,  a mesonet station (RGX-VIrginia Peak) located 8.0 miles SW of Nixon, NV, and a mesonet station (SLIDE) located 1.0 mile ESE of Mount Rose Summit, reported peak wind gusts of 63 mph, 65 mph, and 78 mph.

After a brief break on the afternoon of the 26th, the second storm system passed through the area later in the night on the 26th into the 27th. The first wave brought light Sierra snow and valley rain with winds bringing the main impacts as a belt of amplified southwesterly wind aloft overspread the region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper trough dug south and east into the western US, an attendant cold front swung across northern CA early morning on the 29th and  continued across western NV later that morning through the afternoon. This last in a series of AR supported storm systems brought higher elevation snow, valley rain showers, and strong southwesterly winds to the region. This round of strong southwest winds included gusts up to 40-50 mph in western NV valleys, as well as 120+ mph wind across Sierra ridges. Wind prone areas also saw a 40-60% chance for peak gusts around 60+ mph. ||Impacts included hazardous driving conditions, especially for high profile vehicles, frequent periods of mountain wave turbulence, rotors, and LLWS for aviation concerns, choppy lake conditions on larger area lakes, and periods of blowing dust in dry lake beds/playas for the Basin and Range. Other potential impacts included downed trees, powerlines, and power outages. Blended guidance projected a 40-60% chance for up to 4-8 inches of snowfall at higher passes such as Donner Summit, Echo and Carson Pass. The gusty southwest winds and periods of moderate snow created additional travel challenges over higher mountain passes before tapering off overnight on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported 24 hour storm total snowfall of 12.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This second warmer, and wetter, in a trio of modest storm systems began to impact the Sierra the afternoon of the 13th, and continued through the 14th. It produced its share of travel impacts with periods of heavy snowfall with snowfall accumulation of 15-20 inches through the evening of the 14th. Although there was elevated uncertainty, lower elevation Sierra communities were impacted by 3-6 inches of snow in some areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This next storm system and attendant cold front brought periods of light valley rain and higher elevation snow, gusty winds, along with colder temperatures. The strongest winds from west and northwest occurred overnight on November 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th. Gusts up to 30-40 mph were expected in western Nevada valleys, while Sierra ridges saw winds above 70 mph. The bulk of the rainfall fell across NE California and northern Nevada, with a 70-90% chance for the Sierra crest to accumulate another 1-2 with some terrain favored areas west of Lake Tahoe seeing up to 4 inches of snowfall along the crest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed on the 20th with the approach of an upper level storm system that hovered off the Pacific NW. This allowed for a deep plume of moisture, aka atmospheric river (AR), to be advected towards northern CA for the second half of the week. This enhanced the primary hazard being heavy precipitation across the region with projected liquid storm totals of 2.5-3.0 inches for the Sierra crest, 0.75-1.00 inch for the foothills, and 0.10-0.75 from the Basin and Range into far western Nevada. The secondary hazard that was expected with this system was gusty southwest to south winds. Projected gusts of 30-40 mph in the valleys across the region were complemented with gusts exceeding 70-80 mph across the Sierra crest. Blended guidance showed a 30-50% chance of 50+ mph gusts, while ECMWF Extreme Forecast Index (EFI) also indicated an anomalous event. Other wind impacts included turbulence, hazardous boating conditions, and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported storm total snowfall of 12.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This rounded out a series of 3 AR fed storm systems that arrived late on the 24th, and continued to the 26th. This third, colder, storm system in the series brought another round of precipitation to the Sierra and western Nevada. Early on, this colder system held the best potential for accumulating snowfall. Blended guidance initially showed a 30-40% chance of 3 feet of snow along the Sierra crest, a 25-35% chance of 12 inches of snow in Sierra communities, like Mammoth and South Lake Tahoe, and only a 10-15% chance of an inch of snow for lower western NV areas by early morning on the 23rd. Although the liquid storm totals with this storm were not nearly as impressive as the previous storm system, the colder air, and lower snow levels, allowed for a potential of higher snow totals. Follow-up projections still anticipated the potential for another foot or more of snow above 7000 feet near the Sierra crest with a greater than 75% chance for Mono/Alpine counties, but with less certainty (30-60% chance) around the Tahoe Basin. For Sierra/Tahoe communities around the Lake Tahoe Basin, there was still about a 25-50% chance for at least 4 inches of snowfall. Western NV valleys looked to have mainly light rain showers, with maybe a brief rain-snow mix toward the end of the event on the evening/night of the 26th. Although breezy periods were projected for this storm system, wind projections were not as strong as the previous storm systems. Wind gusts in the 30-45 mph range were forecasted for valley areas with stronger winds over wind prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported storm total snowfall of 8.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern changed on the 20th with the approach of an upper level storm system that hovered off the Pacific NW. This allowed for a deep plume of moisture, aka atmospheric river (AR), to be advected towards northern CA for the second half of the week. This enhanced the primary hazard being heavy precipitation across the region with projected liquid storm totals of 2.5-3.0 inches for the Sierra crest, 0.75-1.00 inch for the foothills, and 0.10-0.75 from the Basin and Range into far western Nevada. The secondary hazard that was expected with this system was gusty southwest to south winds. Projected gusts of 30-40 mph in the valleys across the region were complemented with gusts exceeding 70-80 mph across the Sierra crest. Blended guidance showed a 30-50% chance of 50+ mph gusts, while ECMWF Extreme Forecast Index (EFI) also indicated an anomalous event. Other wind impacts included turbulence, hazardous boating conditions, and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (WASNV-Washoe City-NV DOT)) located 3.2 mi S of Washoe City, NV, mesonet station (NV052-South Verdi) located 2.0 SSE of Verdi, NV, and ASOS station KRNO Reno located 3.0 mi SSW of Sparks, NV, reported peak wind gusts of 76 mph, 71 mph, and 61 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system from the northwest moved over the silver state, along with an associated cold front. Rain/snow showers along the cold front produce some microburst winds which created a few strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (MTHN2) Mather, Elevation 9268 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough and associated cold front moved across northern Nevada during the early morning hours. With front and the northwest aloft, downslope mountain barrier winds from producing very strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (RBVN2) Ruby Valley, Elevation 6891 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fall weather pattern with back-to-back upper level troughs pushed through the northwestern U.S. Between an upper level ridge over the southwest, and the strong trough to the north, a strong pressure gradients resulted across northern Nevada, which in turn helped produce strong gusty winds over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (MPVN2) Martin Creek, Elevation 6439 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fall weather pattern with back-to-back upper level troughs pushed through the northwestern U.S. Between an upper level ridge over the southwest, and the strong trough to the north, a strong pressure gradients resulted across northern Nevada, which in turn helped produce strong gusty winds over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (MORN2) Morey Creek, Elevation 5500 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream crossed over the Mojave Desert, leading to two periods of high winds in the Spring Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These gusts occurred 3 miles NE of Mount Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet stream crossed over the Mojave Desert, leading to two periods of high winds in the Spring Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These gusts occurred 3 miles NE of Mount Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same strong Pacific low pressure system which brought heavy snow to the Sierra on the 25th brought several hours of high winds to the Spring Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These gusts occurred 3 miles NE of Mount Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of mountain snow and wind impacts developed across the Sierra |and western NV as another upper short wave trough, and attendant cold front, |lifted across northern CA late morning on November 13th. This next pre-winter storm system spread light to moderate showers across the region with much of the heavier precipitation staying west of Hwy-395 in Lassen County. Then shower chances increased to the south-southeast into the Lake Tahoe Basin by mid afternoon. This storm system also brought another period of gusty southwest winds to the region as well. Impacts included travel for high profile vehicles on area roadways, increased turbulence for local aviation interests, and elevated fire weather concerns across portions of far western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station WLKNV (NV-DOT) located 6.0 mi NW of Hawthorne, NV, reported  a peak wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This second wave brought higher snow levels with heavy rain and high elevation snow. Although significant rises were anticipated on creeks and streams, no river flooding was forecast. This second round featured more widespread precipitation that reached western Nevada and Mono County. This included greater rain and snowfall totals compared to the first round. Snow levels begin above 7500- 8000' at the onset of precipitation as the region remained situated in the warm air regime. This setup, once again confined snowfall to the High Sierra with rainfall elsewhere. By early on the 23rd a transition into the colder side of the storm lowered projected snow levels down to 6000-7000' that marked the beginning of snowfall down to Sierra passes with travel impacts as well. A foot or two of snow was projected along the Sierra crest during this second round with a 20% chance of highest peaks receiving 3 feet or more of snowfall. Meanwhile, only an inch or less was projected for Sierra communities around the Lake Tahoe Basin. Ensemble simulations also leaned toward a stronger burst of winds ahead of the precipitation. Blended guidance highlighted a 40-50% chance for wind gusts exceeding 50 mph for the afternoon and evening of the 22nd along the Highway 395, and Interstate 580 corridor. These conditions brought impacts to backcountry recreation, hazardous lake conditions, high profile vehicle travel restrictions on area roadways, and aviation turbulence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (GALN2-Galena) located 4.0 NNW of Washoe, NV, a mesonet station (NV052-South Verdi) located 2.0 mi SSE of Verdi, NV, a mesonet station (CAPN2-Carson City) located 2.0 NE of Carson City, NV, and a mesonet station (NV016-Mount Rose Highway) located 2.0 NE of Mount Rose Ski Base, reported peak wind gusts of 73 mph, 77 mph, 92 mph, and 108 mph respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 4.0 S of Sparks, NV, reported a large tree about 3 feet in diameter was blown over along with downed fence sections as well. Estimated gusts up to 80 mph were reported from a home weather station mesonet station. A NWS Employee located 3.0 mi south of Spanish Springs, NV, also reported a downed large tree that measured about 40 feet tall and 2.5 feet diameter.  Event time was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second stronger of a pair of storm systems that advanced into northern California overnight of the 10th through the 11th, brought a short period of gusty southwest winds, mountain snowfall, and valley rain to western NV mostly north of Hwy-50. Although windward areas of the Sierra received up to an inch of liquid precipitation, areas east of the crest also saw up to a half inch of liquid as well. Some terrain favored areas saw Storm total snowfall close to 4-6 inches. Higher Sierra ridgetops saw southwest winds reaching 80+ mph with gusty winds also occurring in more wind prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (RGX-Virginia Peak) located 8.0  mi SW of Nixon, NV, and a mesonet station (SLIDE-Mount Rose Summit) located 1.0 mi ESE of the Mount Rose Summit reported peak wind gusts of 68 mph, and 80 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure from the northwest dropped down towards southwestern U.S. producing precipitation over central and northern Nevada. The passing cold front also helped produce strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (MPVN2) Martin Creek, Elevation 6439 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure from the northwest dropped down towards southwestern U.S. producing precipitation over central and northern Nevada. The passing cold front also helped produce strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station (POCN2) Pole Creek, Elevation 8502 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This rounded out a series of 3 AR fed storm systems that arrived late on the 24th, and continued to the 26th. This third, colder, storm system in the series brought another round of precipitation to the Sierra and western Nevada. Early on, this colder system held the best potential for accumulating snowfall. Blended guidance initially showed a 30-40% chance of 3 feet of snow along the Sierra crest, a 25-35% chance of 12 inches of snow in Sierra communities, like Mammoth and South Lake Tahoe, and only a 10-15% chance of an inch of snow for lower western NV areas by early morning on the 23rd. Western NV valleys looked to have mainly light rain showers, with maybe a brief rain-snow mix toward the end of the event on the evening/night of the 26th. Although breezy periods were projected for this storm system, wind projections were not as strong as the previous storm systems. Wind gusts in the 30-45 mph range were forecasted for valley areas with stronger winds over wind prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station (SCRN2) located 5.0 mi NE of Washoe City, NV, and another mesonet station (LVYN2-Little Valley) located 2.0 mi ESE of Diamond Peak Ski base, NV, reported a 24 hour rainfall totals of 0.94 inches, and 0.52 respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another pre-winter storm system swings another round of snow into the region. Locally heavy snowfall was forecasted near South Lake Tahoe, Carson City, and areas southeast up to and along the Sierra Front. This brought slick roads and travel delays in the Sierra, and some higher elevations of far western Nevada like Virginia City. This time, snow levels dropped close to valley floors Friday that brought a higher chance for a mix of rain and snow across the Reno-Carson City urban area. This also allowed for the 10-20% chance for up to an inch of snow around foothill areas above 5000feet. This system brought minor impacts to the Friday afternoon commute in valley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mount Rose Ski Base reported 24 hour storm total snowfall of 3.0 inches.
